items.q: What does modulation mean in music?
items.correct: Changing from one key to another within a piece.
items.why: Students conflate modulation with any musical change. Specifically, modulation is a KEY CHANGE, usually established by a perfect cadence in the new key - a structural device.

items.q: What does the time signature 6/8 indicate?
items.correct: Two beats per bar, each beat divided into three (compound duple).
items.misconception: Six beats per bar, all equal in stress.
items.why: Students count 6/8 as six equal beats. In practice it's felt as TWO dotted-crotchet beats subdivided into three quavers each - compound duple, not simple sextuple.

items.q: Which chords form a plagal cadence?
items.why: Students confuse plagal (IV-I) with perfect (V-I) because both end on I. The 'amen' at the end of hymns is the classic plagal cadence - subdominant to tonic, with a softer ecclesiastical feel.

items.q: Which recitative type uses only continuo accompaniment?
items.why: Correct answer: Recitativo secco. “Recitativo accompagnato” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Handel's heroic opera roles such as Rinaldo were typically written for which voice type?
items.why: Correct answer: Castrato. “Tenor” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Handel compose 'Rinaldo', his first London opera?
items.why: The real figure is 1711. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
